votes.reason: The Python code correctly checks all possible prefixes of the list, matching the Coq specification's existential and universal quantifiers over prefixes.

The Python code correctly implements the specification by iterating through all possible prefixes (including the empty one) to determine if any sum to less than zero, and verifying that the input result matches this condition.
The Python code correctly checks all prefixes of the list and verifies if any prefix sum is less than zero, perfectly matching the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements sum_list as a fold_left addition starting from 0, and below_zero_spec checks all prefixes (from empty to full list) to determine if any has a negative sum. It returns True iff the given result boolean matches whether such a negative prefix exists, which is exactly what the Coq spec defines: result=true iff some prefix has sum<0, result=false iff all prefixes have sum>=0.

The Python code correctly implements sum_list as a fold-left addition starting from 0, matching Coq's definition. The below_zero_spec function checks whether the result boolean correctly indicates the existence of a prefix with negative sum, which matches the Coq specification's biconditional: result=true iff some prefix has sum<0, result=false iff all prefixes have sum>=0. The Python iterates through all prefixes (including empty and full list) just as Coq's quantification over prefix++suffix decompositions would.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. sum_list computes the same fold_left addition starting from 0. below_zero_spec checks all prefixes (from empty to full list) and returns True iff the result boolean matches whether any prefix has negative sum, which is exactly what the Coq spec defines: result=true iff some prefix has sum<0, result=false iff all prefixes have sum>=0.

Python sums match Coq’s fold_left Z.add and it checks exactly whether result equals the existence of a negative-sum prefix (including empty/full prefixes), which is precisely what the Coq spec states via the two equivalences.

Python sums with unbounded ints and checks if any prefix has negative sum, iterating all prefixes (including empty and full). It returns result == existence_of_negative_prefix, which matches the Coq spec’s two biconditionals.

Python sums integers like Coq Z and checks all prefixes for a negative sum; it returns true exactly when result matches the existence of a negative-prefix, which is equivalent to the Coq biconditionals.
